skill.name: fp-ts-react
skill.description: Practical React patterns for fp-ts, including Option, Either, TaskEither, RemoteData, forms, hooks, and data fetching.
skill.summary: Build type-safe React interfaces with practical fp-ts patterns for state, validation, and async data.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and library references rather than executable skill logic. The Ruby backtick, weak crypto, certificate, reconnaissance, and obfuscation findings are false positives caused by inline TypeScript, tables, and prose. The only real risk indicator is educational fetch usage in sample React code, so publication is acceptable with low risk.

low_findings.title: Educational Fetch Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes React data-fetching examples that call fetch against application API paths. These examples do not exfiltrate data or contact an external host by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls are visible in fenced TypeScript examples and use caller-provided or relative application URLs. No credential access or external collection endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives From Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most reported command execution, cryptography, certificate, reconnaissance, and obfuscation patterns are Markdown tables, inline code names, or TypeScript examples. No executable helper script or prompt injection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review confirms these locations are documentation content such as fp-ts type names, Object.keys usage, npm install text, and a Markdown reference table. The skill file contains no runnable command wrapper.

content.value_statement: React teams often struggle to model missing data, validation errors, and async state safely. This skill gives practical fp-ts patterns for hooks, forms, fetching, and React 19 workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Option, Either, TaskEither, RemoteData, and pipe in React code.
Shows React hook patterns for missing values, async state, and stable fp-ts values.
Provides form validation patterns with Either and accumulated field errors.
Demonstrates TaskEither wrappers for API calls and chained data fetching.
Covers dependency injection with React context and ReaderTaskEither concepts.
Includes React 19 examples for use, useActionState, and useOptimistic.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable tooling or generated project files.
Examples use placeholder functions that must be implemented in the target application.
It assumes users already work with React, TypeScript, and basic fp-ts imports.
It does not provide a complete testing framework or production data layer.

use_cases.title: Model Safer UI State
use_cases.description: Use Option and RemoteData to replace nullable values and conflicting loading flags in React screens.

use_cases.title: Improve Form Validation
use_cases.description: Use Either patterns to return clear field errors and collect multiple validation failures.

use_cases.title: Standardize Async Workflows
use_cases.description: Use TaskEither and typed dependency patterns to make API calls easier to test and compose.

prompt_templates.title: Start With Option
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to replace nullable React state with fp-ts Option in a small component.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design fp-ts Either validation for a signup form with field-level errors.

prompt_templates.title: Compose API Calls
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TaskEither data-fetching pattern for loading a user, posts, and notifications in React.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor State Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React async state design and suggest a RemoteData and dependency injection approach.

output_examples.input: A component stores data, loading, and error as separate state fields.
output: A RemoteData state model with four explicit states.
A rendering plan for not asked, loading, failure, and success views.
Notes about preventing impossible state combinations.

output_examples.input: A signup form validates one field at a time and hides later errors.
output: An Either-based validation strategy that accumulates errors.
A field-error mapping approach for better user feedback.
Guidance on separating validation from submit side effects.

output_examples.input: A React feature calls several APIs in nested effects.
output: A TaskEither composition plan for sequential and parallel requests.
A typed error handling approach for failed requests.
A testable dependency injection outline for API services.

best_practices: Use Option for values that may be absent instead of mixing null and undefined.
Use RemoteData for async UI state when loading, error, and success states must stay consistent.
Memoize fp-ts values or compare them with stable equality when React dependencies matter.
anti_patterns: Do not wrap simple local state in fp-ts types when it makes the component harder to read.
Do not store data, loading, and error booleans separately when impossible combinations can occur.
Do not put side effects inside validation functions that should only return typed results.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or install packages?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill with examples. Users choose whether to apply any package suggestions.

faq.question: Which React versions does it target?
faq.answer: It targets React 18 and React 19 patterns, with notes that also fit modern Next.js projects.

faq.question: Is this only for advanced fp-ts users?
faq.answer: No. It starts with basic Option and Either patterns, then moves into async and dependency patterns.

faq.question: Can it replace a project architecture review?
faq.answer: No. It gives patterns and examples, but teams still need to validate fit for their codebase.

faq.question: Does it cover runtime validation libraries?
faq.answer: It references common libraries such as io-ts and zod, but it does not deeply document them.

faq.question: Why was the audit risk not safe?
faq.answer: The skill includes fetch examples and external library links, so a low risk label is more accurate.
